prompt: How do I check free memory on a Linux server?
ideal: Use free -h for human-readable output. Key metrics: total (all RAM), used (applications), available (for new apps, includes cache). The 'available' column is what matters — Linux uses free RAM for cache/buffers but releases it when apps need it. Don't panic if 'used' is high — check 'available'. For details: cat /proc/meminfo. For per-process: ps aux --sort=-%mem | head.

prompt: How do I set up log rotation for an application?
ideal:
Create a logrotate config in /etc/logrotate.d/myapp:

/var/log/myapp/*.log {
    daily
    rotate 14
    compress
    delaycompress
    missingok
    notifempty
    create 0640 myapp myapp
    postrotate
        systemctl reload myapp >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
    endscript
}

Test: logrotate -d /etc/logrotate.d/myapp (dry-run). Force: logrotate -f /etc/logrotate.d/myapp.

prompt: How do I find which process is consuming the most CPU?
ideal: Use top or htop for real-time monitoring. For scripting: ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -10. For a specific process: pidstat -p PID 1. For historical data: sar -u from the sysstat package. For per-core usage: mpstat -P ALL 1. htop is recommended for interactive use — press F5 for tree view, F6 to sort by CPU.

prompt: How do I configure SSH key-based authentication?
ideal: 1) Generate key: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C 'your@email.com'. 2) Copy to server: ssh-copy-id user@server. 3) Disable password auth: edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config → PasswordAuthentication no, PubkeyAuthentication yes. 4) Reload: systemctl reload sshd. 5) Test: ssh user@server (should not ask for password). Keep private key secure: chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519. Use a passphrase for the key.

prompt: How do I debug a service that's listening on the wrong port?
ideal: 1) Check what's listening: ss -tlnp | grep :PORT. 2) Check service config: grep -r 'PORT' /etc/service/. 3) Check environment variables: systemctl show service -p Environment. 4) Check if another process took the port: lsof -i :PORT. 5) Check firewall: iptables -L -n | grep PORT or ufw status. 6) Check if the service actually started: systemctl status service. 7) Check logs: journalctl -u service -n 50.

prompt: What's the difference between a process and a thread?
ideal: A process is an independent execution context with its own memory space, file descriptors, and PID. A thread shares memory with other threads in the same process. Processes are isolated — one crashing doesn't affect others. Threads share memory — one thread's bug can corrupt another's data. Context switching between processes is more expensive than between threads. Use processes for isolation (gunicorn workers), threads for shared-state concurrency.

prompt: How do I set up automatic security updates on Ubuntu?
ideal: Install: sudo apt install unattended-upgrades. Configure: sudo dpkg-reconfigure -plow unattended-upgrades. Edit /etc/apt/apt.conf.d/50unattended-upgrades for allowed origins. Enable: sudo unattended-upgrades enable. Check logs: cat /var/log/unattended-upgrades/unattended-upgrades.log. Dry-run: sudo unattended-upgrades --dry-run. Recommended: enable security updates automatically, but review and apply regular updates manually.

prompt: How do I monitor disk I/O on a Linux server?
ideal: Use iostat -x 1 for per-device I/O statistics. Key metrics: %util (saturation), await (latency), r/s w/s (throughput). Use iotop for per-process I/O. For historical data: sar -d from sysstat. For specific process: pidstat -d -p PID 1. High %util (>80%) means the device is saturated. High await (>50ms) means I/O latency issues.

prompt: How do I securely expose a local development server to the internet?
ideal: Don't use --host 0.0.0.0 on untrusted networks. Options: 1) SSH tunnel: ssh -L 8080:localhost:8080 user@server. 2) Tailscale/ZeroTier VPN for persistent access. 3) ngrok for temporary HTTPS tunnels (ngrok http 8080). 4) Caddy reverse proxy with authentication. Never bind to 0.0.0.0 without auth — it exposes your service to everyone on the network.

prompt: How do I check if a port is open on a remote server without connecting?
ideal: Use nc (netcat): nc -zv server.example.com 443. -z scans without sending data, -v shows result. For timeout control: nc -zv -w5 server 443 (5 second timeout). Alternative: timeout 5 bash -c 'echo > /dev/tcp/server/443 && echo open || echo closed'. For scanning multiple ports: nmap -p 80,443 server (install nmap first). For SSL check: openssl s_client -connect server:443 </dev/null.

prompt: What is swap space and when should I use it?
ideal: Swap is disk space used when RAM is full. When RAM runs out, the kernel moves least-used pages to swap. Use swap for: 1) Safety net against OOM kills. 2) Occasional memory spikes. 3) Hibernation. Don't use swap as replacement for RAM — disk is 1000x slower. Recommended size: 1x RAM for systems with ≤2GB, 0.5x RAM for 2-8GB, 2GB+ for >8GB. Check: swapon --show. Create: fallocate -l 2G /swapfile && chmod 600 /swapfile && mkswap /swapfile && swapon /swapfile.

prompt: How do I debug a DNS resolution issue?
ideal: 1) Check resolution: dig example.com or nslookup example.com. 2) Check specific DNS server: dig @8.8.8.8 example.com. 3) Flush cache: systemd-resolve --flush-caches (systemd-resolved) or restart nscd. 4) Check /etc/resolv.conf for correct nameservers. 5) Check /etc/hosts for overrides. 6) Trace: dig +trace example.com. 7) Check DNSSEC: dig +dnssec example.com. 8) Verify connectivity: ping -c 1 8.8.8.8.

prompt: How do I create a systemd service for my application?
ideal:
Create /etc/systemd/system/myapp.service:

[Unit]
Description=My Application
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=myapp
Group=myapp
WorkingDirectory=/opt/myapp
ExecStart=/opt/myapp/bin/start
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
Environment=NODE_ENV=production

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

Then: systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl enable myapp && systemctl start myapp

prompt: What's the difference between TCP and UDP?
ideal: TCP: connection-oriented, reliable delivery, ordered packets, flow control, congestion control. Used for web (HTTP), SSH, email. UDP: connectionless, no delivery guarantee, no ordering, no flow control. Used for DNS, DHCP, streaming, gaming. Choose TCP when reliability matters. Choose UDP when speed matters more than reliability or when you implement your own reliability layer (QUIC uses UDP with TCP-like reliability).

prompt: How do I check SSL certificate details from the command line?
ideal: openssl x509 -in cert.pem -text -noout shows all certificate details. For a remote server: echo | openssl s_client -connect domain:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -text. Check specific fields: -subject, -issuer, -dates, -ext subjectAltName. Verify chain: openssl verify -CAfile chain.pem cert.pem. Check expiry days: openssl x509 -checkend 2592000 -in cert.pem (warns if expiring within 30 days).
